Research article
Microstructural evolution and strengthening mechanisms of gas tungsten arc welded nickel-based superalloy joints processed by laser powder bed fusion: Effects of welding direction and heat input
Yaxing Tong a,b
, Guoliang Zhu b,c,*, Sanbao Lin a,**

a State Key Laboratory of Precision Welding & Joining of Materials and Structures, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China b Suzhou Laboratory, No.388, Ruoshui Street, SIP, Jiangsu 215123, China c Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced High-Temperature Materials and Precision Forming, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
A B S T R A C T
Keywords: Nickel-based superalloy Laser powder bed fusion Gas tungsten arc welding Microstructure evolution Mechanical properties

Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) imparts nickel-based superalloys with unique non-equilibrium microstructural features; however, their influence on the welding microstructure evolution and mechanical performance remains insufficiently understood. In this study, autogenous gas tungsten arc welding was applied to precipitation- strengthened ZGH4142 superalloy fabricated by LPBF. Microstructural characterization combined with finite element simulation was used to systematically clarify the effects of heat input and welding direction on the structure and properties of the welded joint. The results revealed that the weld morphology and pool geometry enlarged with increasing heat input, and fine equiaxed grains in the weld center transformed to coarse equiaxed ones. With decreasing cooling rate, the MC carbides coarsened, while the γ′ fraction increased from 14 % to 33 %, shifting the dominant strengthening mechanism from grain refinement to dislocation strengthening. The joints exhibited a typical “M-shaped” hardness profile, with the heat-affected zone (HAZ) and weld zone (WZ) showing the highest and lowest hardness, respectively. Quantitative strengthening analysis indicated that the base metal and WZ were mainly governed by solid-solution strengthening, and the HAZ exhibited predominantly dislocation strengthening. Electron backscattered diffraction analysis further revealed that the grain orientation and low- angle grain boundary fractions were sensitive to the welding direction, reflecting the intrinsic anisotropy of the LPBF-processed alloys. These findings provide fundamental insights and practical guidance for welding evaluation and process optimization of additively manufactured nickel-based superalloys.
1. Introduction

complex microstructures and intrinsic anisotropy may lead to distinct behaviors during subsequent welding and service compared with con­ ventional cast-and-wrought alloys, and a systematic understanding of their weldability remains limited.

Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF [1]), a representative technique of metal additive manufacturing, enables the fabrication of complex structures with high precision owing to its high energy density, rapid solidification, and layer-by-layer deposition [2,3]. The LPBF process imparts unique non-equilibrium microstructural features to alloys [4,5], such as cellular sub-grains, strong crystallographic texture, high dislo­ cation density, and supersaturated solid solution [6,7]. These charac­ teristics enhance the strength [8,9] and high-temperature performance [10] of nickel-based superalloys, extending their potential applications in aerospace [11] and power generation sectors [12,13]. However, such

Nickel-based superalloys [14] are important high-temperature structural materials with extensive applications in aerospace, power generation, chemical processing, etc. Precipitation-strengthened super­ alloys rely on γ′ (Ni3(Al, Ti, Ta)) as the primary strengthening phase [15], which provides excellent creep resistance and long-term stability under elevated temperatures [16]. Conventional weldability assess­ ments typically estimate the hot-cracking susceptibility based on the Al + Ti + Ta content [17], which is valid for cast-and-wrought alloys but

may not accurately describe the welding behavior of LPBF-processed alloys [18]. Recent studies have shown that precipitation-strengthened superalloys such as IN738 [19], CM247LC [20], and CMSX-10 [21], which are generally considered to have poor weldability owing to their high content of γ′-forming element [22], can exhibit improved weld­ ability when fabricated using LPBF [10]. This discrepancy indicates that empirical models derived for conventional alloys may not be directly applicable to additively manufactured alloys, highlighting the urgent need for in-depth investigations on their welding behavior and
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098
microstructural evolution.
theoretical and experimental insights for the weldability assessment and process optimization of additively manufactured superalloys.
2. Experimental procedures
2.1. Materials and welding process

The experimental material was a ZGH4142 nickel-based superalloy plate (200 × 100 × 5 mm³) fabricated using LPBF. The nominal chemical composition of the base metal (BM) is shown in Table 1, and the LPBF process parameters are provided elsewhere Ref. [10]. The GTAW (Fro­ nius MagicWave 4000) was performed in autogenous mode, as sche­ matically illustrated in Fig. 1. Prior to welding, the specimens were mechanically polished to remove surface oxides and ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol.

Studies on the welding behaviors of traditional cast-and-wrought superalloys have mainly focused on hot-cracking susceptibility [23], solidification behavior of the fusion zone (FZ), and optimization of the mechanical properties [24,25]. Strategies to mitigate welding defects typically involve alloy design [26], process parameter adjustment [27], and post-weld heat treatment [28,29]. Nevertheless, the complex non-equilibrium solidification during welding [30], particularly the dynamic evolution and thermodynamic stability of precipitates [31], is not completely understood. Welding of additively manufactured alloys is essential for integrating large-scale components [32], and the reli­ ability of the welded joint is critical to their engineering applications [33]. Gas tungsten arc welding (GTAW), with its moderate heat input and adaptability to complex geometries, has been widely used to pro­ duce high-quality and precision welds [34,35]. However, existing studies are limited to cast-and-wrought alloys [36], and the fusion welding behaviors of LPBF-processed precipitation-strengthened alloys are largely unexplored [37]. The fine-grained microstructure, high dislocation density, and anisotropy of LPBF-processed alloys may lead to entirely different crack susceptibility and microstructural evolution during welding [6].

During the LPBF process, the alloy is subjected to repeated non- equilibrium thermal cycles, which leads to pronounced anisotropy in the microstructure and mechanical properties. Repeated melting and solidification along the BD resulted in distinct grain morphologies compared with those in the SD and TD, making it necessary to investi­ gate the influence of orientation on the weldability and joint performance.

Because autogenous GTAW is generally suitable for thin plates (< 3 mm), the welding parameters were optimized to limit the penetration depth to ~3 mm and prevent excessive melt pool depth or burn-through. The final parameters (Table 2) were determined by adapting the process window of cast-and-wrought nickel-based superalloys [38] to the LPBF plate geometry and microstructural features. The heat input was calculated using the following equation [39]:
Q = UI V η, (1.1)

Therefore, the current knowledge is insufficient to elucidate the welding-induced microstructural evolution and strengthening mecha­ nisms of LPBF-processed precipitation-strengthened nickel-based su­ peralloys, limiting their broader application in advanced service environments. To address this gap, we studied the welding behaviors of LPBF-fabricated ZGH4142 superalloy under different GTAW parame­ ters. The effects of heat input and welding direction on the joint microstructure, precipitation evolution, and mechanical performance were systematically analyzed using multi-scale microstructural charac­ terizations combined with finite element modeling. This study aims to establish the microstructure–property relationships and provide

2.2. Microstructural characterization and mechanical testing of welded joints

Microstructural and compositional analyses were conducted using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; ZEISS GEMINI300) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and electron backscatter diffrac­ tion (EBSD; Oxford Symmetry). MC-type carbides were analyzed at 10 kV and a working distance of 10 mm, while γ′ precipitates were examined at 2 kV and 2 mm. EBSD was performed at 20 kV with a step size of 0.1 µm and a signal-to-noise ratio of 95 %. To minimize surface stress, the samples were electropolished in 10 % perchloric acid and 90 % ethanol at 25 V and −30 ◦C for 30 s. The EBSD data were processed using AztecCrystal to obtain grain orientation, boundary character, and texture. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI Talos 200 ×, 200 kV) was used to characterize the dislocation structures and den­ sities. The TEM foils were prepared using WEDM (0.3 mm thickness), mechanically thinned to ~50 µm, punched into 3 mm disks, and ion- milled (Gatan 695) for ~4 h.

The macroscopic morphology of the welds was first recorded using an optical camera. Metallographic specimens (30 × 5 × 5 mm³) were cut perpendicular to the weld seam by wire electrical discharge machining (WEDM). The samples were mechanically ground and pol­ ished (MECATECH SPC250&300), which was followed by electrolytic etching in a solution of H₂SO₄ (48 mL), HNO₃ (40 mL), and H₃PO₄ (12 mL) at 5 V for 3 s to reveal the microstructures. Optical microscopy (OM; ZEISS Axioscope 5) was used to characterize the weld pool ge­ ometry, including width, penetration depth, and heat-affected zone
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098

The mechanical properties were evaluated using microhardness (FUTURE TECH FM-810) and nanohardness testers (KLA G200X). The hardness mapping covered BM, HAZ, FZ, and weld zone (WZ). The microhardness test was performed at a load of 1000 g and dwell time of 15 s.

elevates the temperature, reduces surface tension, and enhances metal fluidity and arc efficiency, thereby enlarging the weld pool. Excessive heat accumulation may cause overheating or even burn-through; how­ ever, no cracks or significant metallurgical defects are observed in the WZ and HAZ under all the welding parameters, indicating good weld­ ability of the alloy within the selected process window.
2.3. Finite element modeling

To quantitatively evaluate the influence of welding parameters on the weld geometry, the variations in weld width, penetration depth, and HAZ width were studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. With in­ crease in the heat input, higher peak temperatures lead to a significant increase in the weld width (Fig. 3a). In sample B1, the weld width reaches 10.9 mm, penetration depth is 3.3 mm, and the pool area attains a maximum of 27 mm², suggesting enhanced heat transfer efficiency at elevated arc temperatures. The HAZ width exhibits a similar trend to that of the weld width (Fig. 3b), mainly governed by heat input. A higher heat input enhances the arc energy density and arc pressure, which promotes lateral expansion of the weld pool [42]. Interestingly, the width-to-depth ratio (W/P) of the weld pool remains nearly constant at ~2.8 across all the parameters (Fig. 3c), indicating that the weld pool geometry is preserved regardless of the heat input level.

To analyze the thermal and residual stress fields during welding and elucidate the relationship between macro-morphology and microstruc­ tural evolution, a thermo-mechanical finite element model was devel­ oped in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.3. The geometric model (200 × 100 × 5 mm³) was meshed using free tetrahedral elements, with a minimum element size of 0.5 mm in the weld zone, resulting in ~400,000 ele­ ments. The thermal and mechanical boundary conditions were set ac­ cording to Ref. [40], while the thermophysical properties of GH4169 alloy [41] were used as an approximation for ZGH4142. In this model, the LPBF material was treated as an isotropic continuum (neglecting microstructural anisotropy), recoil pressure of metal vapor at high arc temperatures was ignored, and solid-state phase transformations were not considered.
3.2. EBSD analysis of welded joints
3. Results
3.2.1. Grain size and orientation
3.1. Macroscopic morphology of welded joints

Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098

The grain size distributions under different heat inputs are shown in Fig. 5. The average grain size of the BM is 16 μm on the A1–A1 section (Figs. 5e) and 18 μm on the B1–B1 section (Fig. 5f). This difference re­ flects grain coarsening along the scanning direction, as the BM along the A direction experiences more frequent thermal cycles during LPBF. With increasing heat input, the grain size at the weld center along the A di­ rection increases from 56 μm (Fig. 5a) to 105 μm (Fig. 5c), far exceeding that of the BM (Fig. 5e). At identical heat input, the average grain size of the weld along the A direction (94 μm) is slightly higher than that along the B direction (90 μm; Fig. 5b), which may be related to the crystal­ lographic orientation differences in the BM.

At a low heat input (Fig. 6a), the grains preferentially grew along {111}< 110 > , a close-packed orientation in FCC structures charac­ terized by low surface energy, favoring nucleation and stable growth. In the BM, the A1–A1 section exhibits dominant {100}< 101 > orientation (Fig. 6e), whereas the B1–B1 section exhibits mainly {100}< 211 > and {111}< 211 > orientations (Fig. 6f). With an increase in the heat input, the maximum pole density increases from 8.46 to 14.09, indicating stronger texture development (Fig. 6a–c). When the welding direction is changed from B to A, the dominant orientation shifts from {100}< 323 > to {100}< 203 > and {111}< 203 > , suggesting more diverse and dispersed growth orientations. This diversification is likely related to the lower cooling rate of the welds along the A direction (Fig. 9b1, d1), which weakens the dominance of preferred orientations.

3.3. Microstructure of base metal

during cooling induce residual stresses, leading to microstrain [43] and increased dislocation density [44]. Low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs; 2–15◦) are usually associated with dislocation slip [45], whereas high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs; 15–65◦) reflect crystallographic discontinuities and serve as indicators of microstructural stability. At a low heat input, the fraction of LAGBs in the weld center reach 21.7 %, indicating high microstrain caused by rapid cooling. With increasing heat input, the cooling rate decreases, reducing the LAGB fraction to 13.9 %; this suggests relaxation of the microstrain. The FZ exhibits significant local stress concentrations owing to weld shrinkage and BM constraint. In the HAZ, the thermal cycles induce precipitation and coarsening of secondary phases, which slightly increase the microstrain and LAGB fraction compared with those of the BM (Fig. 7a3–d3). A comparison of the BM orientations reveals that the LAGB fraction in the B1–B1 section (48.1 %) is higher than that in the A1–A1 section (39.5 %), confirming more pronounced accumulation of residual strain in the build direction owing to frequent thermal cycling (Fig. 7b, d). Under the same heat input, the welds along the B direction exhibit faster cooling (Fig. 9b1, d1) and higher LAGB fractions in the weld center,

3.4.2. Fusion zone

The SEM and EDS analyses reveal the presence of micropores (Fig. 9a2–d2). Furthermore, the weld microstructure consists of γ-Ni matrix with interdendritic MC carbides enriched in Ta and W (Fig. 9b5–b8). Lower cooling rates promote segregation of low-solubility elements at the interdendritic regions, forming coarse granular or rod- like MC carbides [46] (Fig. 9a3–d3). Meanwhile, γ′ precipitates nucleate and grow rapidly within the supersaturated matrix, forming near-spherical morphologies (Fig. 9a4–d4), consistent with low-energy coherent interface nucleation. The EDS line scans under different cool­ ing conditions (Fig. S1) indicate that segregation patterns are minimally affected by the cooling rate.

The FZ, which is a transitional region between BM and WZ, exhibited microstructural features governed by heat input, cooling rate, and segregation. As shown in Fig. 11, partial dissolution of the cellular structures occur near the fusion line, while the pre-existing nanoscale MC carbides at the cell walls coarsen into submicron particles (Fig. 11a2–d2). Owing to the steep thermal gradients and high cooling rates, the grains adjacent to the fusion line exhibit columnar growth perpendicular to the interface (Fig. 11a1–d1). With an increase in the heat input, the size and volume fraction of the precipitates increase (Fig. 11a4–d4). The EDS line scans across the fusion line (Fig. S2) show minimal differences in the elemental distributions under different pa­ rameters, indicating negligible elemental loss during autogenous GTAW.

Quantitative analysis of the precipitates in the BM adjacent to the FZ (Fig. 12a–b) shows that the size of the MC carbide increases from 0.025 to 0.035 μm² with increase in the heat input. In addition, the size of the γ′ precipitate increases from 16 to 30 nm, and the volume fraction in­ creases from 10 % to 30 %. This coarsening behavior follows the Lif­ shitz–Slyozov–Wagner (LSW) theory [47,48]. A high heat input prolongs the dwell time of the precipitates at high temperature and enhances the diffusion rates, thereby accelerating the coarsening of MC-type carbides and nucleation and growth of γ′ precipitates. The microhardness tests (Fig. 12c) indicated average hardness values of 414, 392.5, 390, and 396 Hv1 for samples A1–D1, respectively. The decrease

in the hardness at lower cooling rates can be attributed to partial dissolution of the cellular structures, which weakens their contribution to dislocation strengthening.

Importantly, the elastic modulus (E) remains nearly constant at ~200 GPa across all the regions. This finding is contrary to the tensile test results reported by Zhou et al. [49], who observed variations in the modulus in the weld regions. The discrepancy may be attributed to methodological differences: nanoindentation involves localized elastic responses, while tensile testing captures the bulk mechanical behavior, which is more sensitive to texture, grain orientation, and residual stress effects.
4. Discussion
3.5. Hardness

Based on the results described in Section 3, the welded joints exhibited distinct gradients in the microstructure and mechanical properties. To elucidate the underlying reason for these variations, the effects of heat input and welding direction on the joint microstructure and the corresponding strengthening mechanisms are discussed below.
4.1. Effects of heat input and welding direction on joint microstructure

The mechanical properties of the welded joints under different heat inputs was investigated via microhardness and nanohardness testing, and the results are shown in Fig. 15. All the joints exhibit a typical “M- shaped” microhardness distribution: lowest in the WZ, highest in the HAZ, and intermediate in the BM (Fig. 15a–d). This distribution is consistent with the microstructural features: coarse grains and reduced dislocation density in the WZ causes low hardness, while dense γ′ pre­ cipitation and higher dislocation density in the HAZ provides significant strengthening. The BM retains fine cellular structures and supersatu­ rated solid solution, exhibiting intermediate hardness. Moreover, the hardness profiles effectively reflect the weld width and HAZ width, consistent with the metallographic observations.

To further understand the mechanical properties of the microstruc­ tural regions, nanoindentation tests were performed on BM, HAZ, FZ, and WZ under the B1 condition (Fig. 15e–h). The load–displacement curves (Fig. 15f) confirm that the HAZ exhibits the highest nano- hardness (4.9 GPa) whereas the WZ exhibits the lowest (4.2 GPa), matching well with the microhardness distribution (Fig. 15h).

The microstructural evolution of the welded joints is strongly gov­ erned by the welding thermal cycle. According to the cooling rate model, V = G × R, where V is the cooling rate, G is the thermal gradient, and R is the solidification rate (R remains nearly constant at the weld center). Thus, increases in the heat input enlarged the molten pool (Fig. 2a–c) primarily by reducing the thermal gradient, which in turn decreased the cooling rate [50]. Furthermore, under identical welding parameters, welds along the A direction exhibited larger molten pools compared with those along the B direction (Fig. 3a–b). This difference likely originated from the anisotropy introduced during LPBF; the A direction experienced more thermal cycling, leading to distinct grain boundary features (Fig. 4b4, d4) and microstructural anisotropy (Fig. 8a1, b1).
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098

The reduced cooling rates at higher heat inputs promoted growth of γ′ precipitates and increased their volume fraction (Fig. 10b) together with significant grain coarsening (Fig. 10c). Growth of γ′ precipitates is diffusion-controlled, and higher cooling rates (greater undercooling) suppress Al diffusion, thereby limiting γ′ coarsening. In contrast, the average grain size of the HAZ remained similar to that of the BM (Fig. 4a3–c3), although the sizes and fractions of the MC carbides and γ′ precipitates slightly increased (Fig. 14a–b), suggesting that the welding thermal cycles promoted local precipitation evolution in this region.

(Fig. 16a). The molten pool geometry was consistent with the experi­ mental observations (Fig. 16b–c), validating the model. The tempera­ ture gradient decreased outward from the weld center (Fig. 16d–e), and the cooling rate (~80 K/s) matched the value estimated from the dendrite arm spacing (Fig. 16f). In terms of stress distribution, tensile residual stresses (~600 MPa) were formed in the weld center, while compressive stresses (~160 MPa) were concentrated in the HAZ (Fig. 16g), consistent with the results of Stone et al. [53]. The residual stresses may induce localized strain hardening, thereby contributing to joint strengthening.

4.2. Strengthening mechanisms of welded joints

The welding direction is another important factor that affects the microstructural features. When welding is performed along the A di­ rection, the grains in the weld center are oriented toward < 110 > (Fig. 4b1), while the B-direction welding favors < 111 > orientation (Fig. 4d1). Since the < 110 > orientation is closer to the primary heat flow direction < 100 > and possesses lower interfacial energy [51], the welds along the A direction exhibited larger average grain sizes (Fig. 5b, d). Correspondingly, lower cooling rates in the A direction promoted the formation of coarser MC carbides (Fig. 9b3, d3) and larger γ′ precipitates with higher volume fractions (Fig. 9b3, d3). Contrarily, the HAZ grain size remained close to that of the BM (Fig. 4d3–d4), and precipitation evolution followed similar trends (Fig. 13b1–d2).

To further clarify the mechanisms of macro- and microstructural evolutions, finite element modeling of autogenous GTAW under the B1 condition (1 J/mm) was performed using a Gaussian surface heat source (Eq. 1.4 [52]). The simulated temperature field exhibited an elliptical distribution on the BM surface, with a peak temperature of ~2150 K

The hardness distribution (Fig. 15) highlighted that the HAZ exhibited the highest hardness, underscoring the critical role of γ′ pre­ cipitation in strengthening. The precipitation of γ′ is controlled by the nucleation barrier (interfacial energy and chemical driving force) and elemental diffusion [54]. During LPBF, the extremely high heating and cooling rates (10⁵–10⁷ K/s) substantially increased the effective nucle­ ation barrier while suppressing the diffusion of Al and Ta, resulting in a
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098

supersaturated γ matrix with negligible γ′ precipitation [55]. Fig. 17 shows the microstructural evolution pathway of the LPBF-processed ZGH4142 alloy during GTAW thermal cycling. The BM retained fine

cellular structures and high dislocation density (Fig. 17b). The HAZ, which was subjected to sub-solidus peak temperatures (~1220 ◦C [10]) and slower cooling, entered the γ′ precipitation time–temperature
Y. Tong et al. Journal of Materials Processing Tech. 346 (2025) 119098
where k is the material constant (750 MPa⋅μm1/2[62]), and d is the average grain size.

window [56], leading to abundant spherical γ′ precipitates (Fig. 17c). The regions near the fusion line, which approached the alloy liquidus (~1383 ◦C), exhibited partial dissolution of cellular structures and coarsening of MC carbides and γ′ precipitates (Fig. 17d). However, the weld center, which underwent complete melting and resolidification, cooled within the precipitation window of γ′ (and MC), thus facilitating their simultaneous nucleation and growth (Fig. 17e). Rapid cooling relative to vapor escape also led to pore formation [57].

Dislocation strengthening was estimated using the Taylor relation (Eq. 1.7 [63]). The TEM results showed dislocation densities of 2.1 × 10 ¹ ⁴ m⁻², 6.5 × 10 ¹ ⁴ m⁻², and 0.25 × 10 ¹ ⁴ m⁻² for BM, HAZ, and WZ, respectively (Fig. 18). The corresponding strengthening con­ tributions were 454, 780, and 153 MPa (Fig. 19b). The high strength of the HAZ indicated its increased dislocation density, consistent with its higher fraction of LAGBs (Fig. 7).
σdis = αMGbρ
where α is a geometric constant (0.5), M is the Taylor factor (3.06), G is the shear modulus (80 GPa), b is the length of the Burgers vector (2.5 × 10−10 m), and ρ is the dislocation density.

ZGH4142 superalloy, with its high Al and Ta content, is a typical precipitation-strengthened alloy [58]. The joint strength is derived from solid-solution strengthening (σss), dislocation strengthening (σdis), and grain-boundary strengthening (σHP), expressed as Eq. (1.5) [59]. Under the B1 condition, the yield strengths of BM, HAZ, and WZ, as calculated from the nanoindentation load–displacement data (Fig. 15e) using the Oliver–Pharr and Dao methods [60], were 1201, 1461, and 1007 MPa, respectively.
σs = σss + σdis + σHP (1.5)

Solid-solution strengthening (σss) was obtained by subtracting the grain-boundary and dislocation contributions from the yield strength. The results showed σss values of 559, 504, and 777 for BM, HAZ, and WZ, respectively (Fig. 19c).

Grain-boundary strengthening follows the Hall–Petch relationship (Eq. 1.6 [61]). Based on the grain sizes from Fig. 4 (b1, b3–b4), the average grain sizes of BM, HAZ, and WZ were 16, 18, and 95 μm, cor­ responding to strengthening contributions of 188, 177, and 77 MPa, respectively (Fig. 19a). Thus, the coarse-grained WZ exhibited markedly reduced Hall–Petch strengthening.

contributions of solid solution and grain-boundary strengthening mechanisms were 34 and 12 %, respectively. According to the Orowan mechanism, γ′ and MC precipitates also pinned the dislocations, further enhancing the strength [64], [65]. In the WZ, solid-solution strength­ ening dominated (77 %), whereas dislocation (15 %) and grain-boundary strengthening (8 %) were limited owing to significant grain coarsening (Fig. 5b) and reduced dislocation density (Fig. 18a).
Appendix A. Supporting information

References

Overall, the LPBF base metal exhibited high strength owing to su­ persaturated solid solution and high dislocation density; the weld center exhibited strength loss due to grain coarsening and dislocation annihi­ lation; and the HAZ exhibited the highest strength through γ′ precipi­ tation and dislocation strengthening. This reveals the unique strengthening mechanisms of the LPBF-processed alloys: BM and WZ are dominated by solid-solution strengthening and HAZ is dominated by dislocation strengthening.
5. Conclusions

This study systematically investigated the microstructural evolution and strengthening mechanisms of LPBF-fabricated ZGH4142 nickel- based superalloy joints welded by autogenous GTAW under different heat inputs. The main conclusions are as follows:

1. The LPBF-produced ZGH4142 alloy exhibited fine grains, high dislocation density, and cellular substructures, forming a supersat­ urated solid solution. Distinct thermal cycles during welding induced zone-specific microstructural evolution: HAZ consisted of cellular structures (retained below their dissolution temperature) with γ′ precipitation; fusion zone exhibited partial dissolution of cells accompanied with coarsening of MC carbide; and the weld center underwent complete melting and re-solidification, resulting in coarse equiaxed grains and reduced dislocation density. 2. The heat input and welding direction significantly influenced the joint microstructures. Increasing the heat input decreased the cool­ ing rate, promoting grain coarsening, γ′ growth and enrichment, and coarsening of MC carbide. The welds along the A direction exhibited lower cooling rates, larger grain sizes, and more extensive precipi­ tation of MC and γ′ phases, highlighting the inherent anisotropy of the LPBF-processed materials. 3. Mechanical testing revealed a characteristic “M-shaped” hardness distribution across the joint: highest in the HAZ, intermediate in the BM, and lowest in the WZ. Strengthening in the HAZ resulted from γ′ precipitation and increased dislocation density, whereas the WZ exhibited the lowest hardness because of grain coarsening and reduced dislocation density. 4. Quantitative strengthening analysis indicated that the BM strength was dominated by solid-solution strengthening (47 %), supple­ mented by dislocation and grain-boundary contributions. In the HAZ, dislocation strengthening accounted for the largest fraction (53 %) owing to γ′ precipitation and dislocation accumulation. In contrast, the WZ was primarily strengthened by solid solution (77 %), while dislocation and grain-boundary contributions were limited.

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Schematic of the autogenous gas tungsten arc welding of laser powder bed fusion-produced ZGH4142 superalloy. Direction A corresponds to the build direction, direction B is the scanning direction, and direction C corresponds to the thickness direction. The red and blue dashed boxes indicate welding along di­ rections A and B, respectively.

Fig. 2. Surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the welds under different welding parameters: (a) A1, (b) B1, (c) C1, and (d) D1. The red dashed area corresponds to the weld zone, area between the red and blue dashed lines indicate the heat-affected zone, and outside region is the base metal.
Fig. 3. Variation of the weld dimensions with the welding parameters: (a) weld width and area, (b) heat-affected zone width and area, (c) weld penetration depth and width-to-depth ratio.
Fig. 4. Inverse pole figure maps of the welded joints: (a1–a3) A1, (b1–b4) B1, (c1–c3) C1, and (d1–d4) D1. The black solid lines denote high-angle grain bound­ aries (>15◦).
Fig. 5. Grain size distributions of the welds under different conditions: (a) A1, (b) B1, (c) C1, and (d) D1. Base metal cross-sections of: (e) A1–A1 plane and (f) B1–B1 plane.

Fig. 2 shows the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the welded joints in the LPBF-produced ZGH4142 alloy under different welding parameters. At a low heat input (Fig. 2a), the insufficient energy density limits arc heating, which reduces the weld pool width and depth and results in a narrow FZ. When the welding speed is decreased (Fig. 2c), the weld pool gradually widens. The rapid conduction of heat into the non-preheated BM in the initial stage restricts melt pool expansion. As the welding progresses, the localized heat accumulation

Fig. 4 presents the grain orientation features of the welded joints under different welding parameters. At a low heat input, the weld center mainly consists of fine equiaxed grains (Fig. 4a1), whereas the FZ ex­ hibits a mixed structure of columnar and cellular grains (Fig. 4a2). The HAZ and BM retain fine cellular microstructures (Fig. 4a3). With an increase in the heat input (Fig. 4b1–c1), higher arc temperatures enhance the degree of metal melting, which significantly coarsens the equiaxed grains in the weld center. The welding direction also has a pronounced effect on the grain orientation (Fig. 4d1–d4), which mani­ fests as preferential growth of grains along the normal direction to the
Fig. 6. Pole figures and inverse pole figures of the welds and base metal: (a) A1, (b) B1, (c) C1, and (d) D1. Base metal cross-sections of: (e) A1–A1 plane and (f) B1–B1 plane.

Fig. 7. Grain boundary misorientation maps of the welded joints: (a1–a3) A1, (b1–b4) B1, (c1–c3) C1, and (d1–d4) D1. The blue and red lines indicate low-angle (2–15◦) and high-angle grain boundaries (>15◦), respectively.
Fig. 7 shows the grain boundary characteristics under the different welding parameters. Thermal expansion during heating and contraction

Fig. 8. Microstructural features of the laser powder bed fusion-produced base metal: (a1) Optical microscopy (OM) image, (a2–a3) High-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, (a4–a6) Corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) maps, and (a7) EDS line scan results in (a2). (b1) OM image, (b1–b3) High-magnification SEM images, (b4–b6) EDS maps, and (b7) EDS line scan results in (b2).

Fig. 8 shows the microstructural characteristics of the LPBF- produced ZGH4142 alloy. The OM images show that the A1–A1 sec­ tion is dominated by columnar grains (Fig. 8a1), whereas the B1–B1 section exhibits a typical fish-scale-like melt pool morphology (Fig. 8b1). The SEM and EDS analyses show that Ta and C segregate along the cell walls, forming submicron-scale MC carbides, whereas no γ′ precipitates are detected within the cell interiors (Fig. 8a2–a6, b2–b6). The EDS line scans further confirm that Ni, Al, Ta, and C exhibit similar distribution patterns across the A1–A1 and B1–B1 sections (Fig. 8a7, b7). These results suggest that the extremely high cooling rate during LPBF suppresses γ′ nucleation and growth, leading to the formation of a supersaturated solid solution.

Fig. 9. Microstructure of weld centers under different heat inputs: (a1–d1) Optical microscope images showing mixed dendritic and equiaxed grains; (a2–d2) Low- magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing the presence of small pores at the interdendritic regions; (a3–d3, a4–d4) High-magnification SEM images showing coarse MC carbides in the interdendritic areas and spherical γ′ precipitates in γ matrix. (b5–b8) Corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy maps in (b2) confirm γ-Ni matrix with MC carbides.

Fig. 10. Segregation and precipitation under different cooling rates: (a) Partition coefficient k of the alloying elements; (b) Size and volume fraction of γ′; (c) Average grain size; and (d) Average microhardness (the error bars represent one standard deviation (1σ)).

Fig. 10a shows partition coefficients (k = Cinter/Cintra) of the major alloying elements. Ni and Cr exhibited k ≈1, suggesting uniform dis­ tribution; Co and W preferentially segregate inside the dendrites (k > 1), whereas Mo, Ta, and Al are concentrated in the interdendritic regions (k < 1). The cooling rate exerts limited influence on the segregation, indicating that the solid–liquid partition coefficients play a dominant role. Quantitative analysis (Fig. 10b–c) showed that the size of the γ′ precipitate increases from 20 to 49 nm, and its volume fraction increases from 14 % to 33 % with decrease in the cooling rate, while the average grain size increases from 56 to 105 μm. Since γ′ strengthening primarily originates from dislocation shearing and bypassing mechanisms, this coarsening enhances precipitation strengthening. The microhardness tests (Fig. 10d) show average hardness values of 427, 401, 400, and 384 Hv1 for samples A1–D1, respectively. The initial decrease followed by

Fig. 11. Microstructure of the fusion zone: (a1–d1) Low-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing columnar grain growth near the fusion line; (a2–d2, a3–d3, and a4–d4) High-magnification SEM images showing coarsening of nanoscale MC carbides to submicron particles and γ′ precipitation. (b5–b8) Corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy maps in (b2) confirm uniform elemental distribution.

Fig. 13 shows the microstructural evolution of the HAZ under different heat inputs. Compared with the FZ, the HAZ experiences lower peak temperatures and shorter dwell times, which prevents extensive dissolution of the cellular structures. Consequently, the MC carbides remain nanoscale, and the γ′ precipitates show only moderate increases in size and fraction (Fig. 13a2–d2).

Fig. 12. Precipitation near the fusion zone at different cooling rates: (a) Average area of MC carbide; (b) Size and volume fraction of γ′; and (c) Average micro­ hardness (the error bars represent one standard deviation (1σ)).

Fig. 13. Microstructural features of the heat-affected zone: (a1–d1) Low-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing cellular structure largely preserved with slight local dissolution; (a2–d2) High-magnification SEM images showing minor coarsening of MC carbides and γ′.

Fig. 14. Precipitation in the heat-affected zone under different cooling rates: (a) Average area of the MC carbide; (b) Size and volume fraction of γ′; and (c) Average microhardness (the error bars represent one standard deviation (1σ)).

Fig. 14 shows the results of quantitative evolution of the γ′ pre­ cipitates. As the heat input increases, the size of the MC carbide in­ creases from 0.005 to 0.025 μm² (Fig. 14a). Furthermore, the size of the γ′ precipitate increases from 15 to 31 nm and volume fraction increases from 6 % to 20 % (Fig. 14b). The microhardness tests (Fig. 14c) indicate stable hardness values ranging from 426 to 434 Hv1 across all condi­ tions, suggesting that the thermal effects in the HAZ are relatively similar to those in the FZ. Thus, the precipitation strengthening in the γ matrix is maintained by comparable dislocation strengthening contributions.
Fig. 15. Hardness distribution of the welded joints under different parameters: (a) A1; (b) B1; (c) C1; (d) D1; and (e–h) Corresponding nanoindentation tests results for B1.

Fig. 16. Finite element simulation of autogenous gas tungsten arc welding and comparison with experimental results: (a) Three-dimensional temperature field; (b–c) Simulated liquid zone agrees with the metallographic weld shape; (d) Temperature field of the A1–A1 section; (e) Stress distribution of the A1–A1 section; (f) Variation in the temperature of point A with time; (g) Temperature and stress along line A.

Fig. 17. Schematic of the microstructural evolution in laser powder bed fusion-processed ZGH4142 superalloy during autogenous gas tungsten arc welding: (a) Base metal: cellular structure, fine grains, and high dislocation density; (b) Heat-affected zone: retained cell walls with spherical γ′ precipitation; (c) Fusion zone: partial dissolution of cells with coarsened MC carbides and γ′ phase; (d) Weld center: complete melting and resolidification leading to coarse equiaxed grains and large precipitates.

Fig. 18. Dark-field transmission electron microscopy images of the different zones under B1 welding condition: (a) Weld zone: lowest dislocation density (~0.25 × 10 ¹⁴ m⁻²); (b) Heat-affected zone: highest dislocation density (~6.5 × 10 ¹⁴ m⁻²); (c) Base metal: intermediate density (~2.1 × 10 ¹⁴ m⁻²).

Fig. 19. Strengthening contributions of different mechanisms under B1 welding condition: (a) Grain boundary strengthening; (b) Dislocation strengthening; (c) Solution strengthening; (d) Combined contribution: base metal dominated by solid solution (47 %), heat-affected zone dominated by dislocations (53 %), and weld zone dominated by solid solution (77 %).

Fig. 19d shows the contributions of different strengthening mecha­ nisms. In the BM, solid-solution strengthening was dominant (47 %), followed by dislocation (38 %) and grain-boundary strengthening (16 %), reflecting its supersaturated γ matrix (Fig. 8a7, b7), high dislocation density (Fig. 18c), and fine grains (Fig. 4b4). In contrast, dislocation strengthening dominated the HAZ (53 %) owing to γ′ pre­ cipitation (Fig. 13b2) and increased dislocation density (Fig. 18b). The
